author: @kadmow
text:
For General info. - the additional "control" in a kettle (and many other devices - for ultimate appliance fire safety)  a thermal fuse, when it blows, you may as well spend the $6-20 and buy a new kettle -- unless you know why it failed (around $4 from your electrinics supply for the fuse) in which case replace the fuse and hope all is ok...  lol...

Re.. heater types - have you covered PTC ceramic heaters - cheap,  quick acting  and "self regulating"

Warning - many of the Chinese PID thermal controllers run iff 240V AC - full mains current, and the SSRs pacared allegedly are of dubious provenance - I tend to buy a full price locally sourced DC - rated SSR for projects such as 3d Printer heated beds or other heating projects.
(noting that 12V rated heater pads run perfectly well off 18V  RC battery charger power supplies - -probably the lowest cost "reliable 600-1000W power supply.  (I also sourced 240V rated heating pads for projects to free up my precious DC PSU)

author: @tysondundas1947
text: Electric companies are only interested in total demand relating to the cable size into the installation. They will contol the amps by the size of fuse they install at the origin of your supply. They aren't at all interested in the size or fuses/ breakers with in the installation. NB with LED lighting a 6amp fuse is way over sized and a 2 or 3Amp fuse would be more than sufficient. Gls Lamps "light/lamp bulbs" have not been sold for general use in the uk for 12 years plus.

author: @fyremoon
text: For those who want to DIY their heater controls there are plenty of Arduino projects out there that can use a thermocouple as the temperature measurement and an SSR to switch the heater on and off. Thermocouples have two dissimilar metals and are joined at one end. As the temperature changes, this creates a tiny voltage difference in the thermocouple and an interface chip converts that change into data that can be read easily.

author: @robertgaines-tulsa
text: In North America, homes have a dual voltage system. More specifically, homes are supplies with two 120 volt lines that are 180 degrees out of phase and a centered tapped neutral from the transformer. General receptacles and lighting circuits us either on of the pair of 120 volt lines and a neutral. Major appliances like a water heater or range (cooker) are ran on both pairs of 120 volt lines for a total of 240 volts. Homes generally have either 15 or 20 amp circuits. Often, lighting circuits will be 15 amp, but they also can be 20 amp. 240 lines typically range from 15, 20, 30, 40, and 50 amps. The 50 amp is more for the main breaker for the 120 volt circuits in the circuit breaker panel. In the case of 240 volt breakers, they are actually composed of two 120 volt lines that are 180 degrees out of phase, and they are bridged together so both trip in a fault condition. Newer breakers are starting to have one lever that controls both lines.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Great video. Some comments on it :
As for heat and heat loss you have pretty much explained newtons law of cooling. When something is heated it also gives heat to its environment. If both reach equilibrium the temperature remains static. More or less that is because it cannot ever be reached. The formula for it is the same as the formula for charging a capacitor. You will get very close to the charging voltage but never reach it.
There is a thermal version of ohms law. Pretty much the same law but for temperature. It allows for calculating the temperature for a semiconductor for example on a heat sink. The heat sink has a thermal resistance usually given in Kelvin per Watt.
As for AC or DC makes a difference on a heating element : The mains voltage is given as effective voltage or RMS. That is, this AC voltage will give the same power in a resistive load as the DC voltage. It's the same per definition. Literally. In modern times however with led lamp and cfl lamps a resistive load is rare and power will differ for the same voltage DC or AC ( if it works at all ). One more thing I would like to mention is although ohms law is simple one could be surprised how difficult it can become if you have a network of resistors. It can be extremely complex to calculate something in a complex network although ohms law is is the only thing relevant.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are right resistive loads on lighting are rare but resistive loads themselves aren't. I only used the filament bulb as an example of a resistive load not as an example of lighting - what I said applies to resistive loads only!

author: @Xero1of1
text: 4:52 Gotta interject here. This is where comparing electricity to fluids fails. Amperage isn't the number of electrons whipping past... electrons don't actually move all that much... mostly from one atom to another, dislodging a spare electron which then collides into another atom. Sort of like billiards. The number of these electrons moving at once from one atom to the next, in the same direction, is what current is. If you were to track a single electron's path in a circuit, it'd only move a couple microns... but the electric field that that movement creates does travel from one end to the other. I think the most apt depiction of electricity is one of those desk art pieces with a bunch of balls suspended from above. Lift up one ball on the end and let it smack back down into place, and the ball on the other side of the line of balls moves out, then back in, smacking into place and dislodging the ball on the other end again. The balls in the middle which don't really move but still transfer the force between them is how electricity works. The force transferred is the electric field, which actually does the work inside the circuit. The harder the balls, the less resistance and the more power that can be transferred, the softer the balls, the higher the resistance and the less power can be transferred.

replies.author: @Xero1of1
replies.text: 6:44 This also helps you to understand why some materials have higher or lower resistance than others. The electric field and current depend on the direction of movement of these electrons as they're smacking into atoms. The more chaotic the structure of the material is, the less chance there will be to have more electrons moving in the same direction, so you get higher resistance. The more crystalline the structure, as in most metals, the easier it is to move electrons in the same direction, which leads to less resistance and higher amperage... instead of bouncing around in all directions creating a bunch of heat that won't go anywhere, most of them bounce in a single direction... which is what you want for electrical transmission.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: so you are not a fan of the 'sea of electrons' model for metals then? To be honest mate - I don't disagree agree with you particularly but I will say we really don't know how this stuff works - we have a few models of how we think it works and those models fit and describe behaviour we see and we then use those models to predict but that is different from knowing. The complexity fo a model is really down the complexity of the system we are analysing as opposed to anything being wrong or right per se

author: @jamest.5001
text: It's good to incorporate a normally closed thermal switch close to the heat source, with a temp rating, just below the max safe operating temperature, to shut it off before it burns down something! I use them with temperature controllers,   just Incase the relay sticks,  or the electronics fail in the on position,   a fail safe! It will keep operating at the max temperature, so a thermal fuse would not be a bad idea either!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it really does depend mate - if you want to hold up your trousers with a belt, braces and a piece of string - go right ahead - but quite a lot of the time there is simply no need which is why in so many commercial products you just don't see them. Of course you always see a fuse - that's never a bad idea and ultimately is the most well used safety device and really should never be omitted in something you build yourself

Like the  Inkbird ITC306T.

author: @CarstenBukholdtAndersen
text: Excellent channel! Technically the electrons do not "zip by" - see "drift velocity". The charge is carried as an electromagnetic wave. But good analogy none the less.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate technically we have no idea if they zip or not - we do have models that explain behaviour and help predict - but that's about it - the wave model you suggest is great for different systems but the particle model and 'sea of electrons' idea also helps - to me the model applied is about the teaching point we want to make and the system we want to analyse more than it being right or wrong per se

author: @joelaichner3025
text: Same with your gas tank , in tank electric fuel pump , never let your tank get below a 1/4 of a tank , pump runs hotter , then locks up ,

replies.author: @551moley
replies.text:
I've had two vehicles running on LPG, one van and one car, both had fuel pumps in the tank, I did over 100,000 in each, very very rarely was there more than a 1/4 of a tank of petrol, normally just enough to keep the level light off, both were EFI and only the injectors were turned off (emulators are used to intercept the signal, keeping   the ECU happy) 
So in both cases the fuel pumps was running continuously never had any problems, I gave a mate a lift from Cornwall to Telford every week for months, he was very worried we wouldn't make it with the fuel light on! We stopped in Bristol and I put £5 worth of petrol in (5lts ish at the time) he was amazed at the fuel economy when the light was still off 400 miles later on with only a £5 worth in the tank?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @joelaichner3025
replies.text: Things in America , designed to fail after car warranty expires

author: @joelaichner3025
text: Rule of thumb , only draw 75 percent of breaker capacity , 20 amp breaker , 2000 watts , only pull 1500 watts continuously

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's great help mate and thank you for it - but that really depends - mostly on how you installed the wire from the breaker to the outlet

author: @roystonbarton
text: why do we need neutral..??? if we are just useing resistance....where does neutral go????

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: Only two terminal connections are needed to get electric current to flow through a material. The current flows between live and neutral.  Usually the ground connection is at the same potential as neutral and added as a safety feature in circuits.

replies.author: @roystonbarton
replies.text: @William_Hada DOH..........

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think William explained for you mate

author: @brokenbravo83
text: Grew up loving bill Nye the science guy, but no longer a fan of his... You sir, need to have more recognition for this style of explaining things. I've binge watched so many of your videos that algorithm is even confused. Outstanding work sir, amazing demeanor, I wish I could be your shadow for a while just to absorb whatever knowledge I could. Keep up the inspirational work!!!

replies.author: @toniwilson6210
replies.text: Why don’t you like Bill Nye anymore? I don’t see how you can compare this program to Bill Nye. No kid would (or should) watch these videos.

replies.author: @brokenbravo83
replies.text: @toniwilson6210 because it sparks my interest with how things work and operate. I watched one of his videos with my son and we built it together. How is this not suitable? My boy went from not knowing anything about a topic to understanding how it works, why it works, and can now build one himself

replies.author: @toniwilson6210
replies.text: @brokenbravo83 Firstly, you didn’t answer my main question…. Sigh… I anticipated that you would try to shift the focus. Unfortunately, I can’t say that I would never let a kid watch these videos. I would recommend that kids watch them with supervision, because kids do not have a fully formed understanding of the consequences of playing with fire or electricity. My primary point is that these videos and the Bill Nye show are just fundamentally different. No equivalency comparison is possible.

replies.author: @brokenbravo83
replies.text: @toniwilson6210 what main question and focus are you speaking of? I answered your question. Yes I compared him to bill Nye as bill Nye inspired my love of science and eventually got my engineering degree. My son is following my footsteps and is becoming inspired by being intrigued. I have over 125 certifications now and enjoy watching my son learn with the same enthusiasm I once had.

replies.author: @krishampton8163
replies.text: @brokenbravo83 leave it be, he's just being argumentative and trying to pull you into it as well. Waste of time. Glad to hear that you're inspiring your kid though, we need more mechanically inclined problem solvers in the world.
